query_text: Does HTTP/3 run over QUIC on UDP, and what does that change compared with HTTP/2?
rationale: Four models unanimously state settled protocol facts (HTTP/3 = HTTP over QUIC, QUIC on UDP) and every inline ordinal indexes a real row of its OWN slot bibliography: slot 1 [1]/[2] against 2 sources, slots 2/4 [1][2][3] against 3 sources, slot 3 [1][2] against 2. Slot 2 also carries an on-run RFC 9000 markdown link and the synthesis cites RFC 9114/9000/9113 by markdown link, all of which resolve against the run-wide URL set. Content-derived expectation: 21 ordinals + 4 on-run URL markers = 25 markers, ALL resolve, so grounding = 25/25 = 1.0 (>= 0.8 GROUNDING_GOOD_THRESHOLD) -> faithful, low risk. live_ratio 1.0 (all openrouter_search), completeness 1.0 (all completed). No high_stakes_notice and quality_checks.high_stakes_warning_required=false -> both HS flags false. Uncertainty section > 20 chars -> uncertainty_surfaced true. No polar pair split (no lone yes/no/safe/true/etc.) -> polar false, disagreement_suppressed false. This is a structural grounding claim, not a subject-matter one; the protocol facts are textbook-verifiable so no operator label is needed. AUDIT: verified against the real engine (grounding 1.0, faithful, low). Corrected the rationale's marker count from the draft's '21 ordinals' to the engine's true 25/25 (the 4 resolving on-run URL markers were previously uncounted); band and labels unchanged.

initial_answers.answer_text:
## What HTTP/3 actually runs on

HTTP/3 is HTTP semantics carried over QUIC, and QUIC is a **UDP-based** transport rather than a TCP one [1]. The mapping document is explicit that stream multiplexing, per-stream ordering and connection migration are provided by the transport and are not re-implemented at the HTTP layer [1].

The transport specification itself defines loss recovery, congestion control and the cryptographic handshake, with TLS 1.3 folded *into* the handshake rather than layered above it [2].

- Head-of-line blocking that came from a single TCP connection is removed per stream, because each QUIC stream is independently ordered [2].
- The HTTP framing layer is thinner as a result: framing that existed only to emulate multiplexing is gone [1].

One honest limit: this describes the published specifications, not what any particular CDN or origin has deployed.

sources.title: RFC 9000: QUIC: A UDP-Based Multiplexed and Secure Transport

initial_answers.answer_text:
### Short version

Same conclusion. The HTTP/3 specification defines the mapping of HTTP semantics onto QUIC [1], where the earlier version mapped the same semantics onto a single TCP connection with its own framing and `HEADERS`/`DATA` frames [2]. The transport document defines QUIC as running on UDP with its own reliability and congestion control [3].

Things worth stating precisely:

1. Discovery is usually via the `Alt-Svc` header or the HTTPS DNS record — a client does not simply guess [1].
2. Header compression changed: QPACK replaces HPACK precisely because QUIC streams can arrive out of order relative to each other [1].
3. Reliability is per stream, so one lost datagram stalls one stream instead of the whole connection [3].

> See the transport document at [RFC 9000](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9000) for the normative wording on the handshake.

I am less certain about how much of the measured latency win in public benchmarks comes from the transport itself as against co-deployed tuning.

sources.title: RFC 9000: QUIC: A UDP-Based Multiplexed and Secure Transport

initial_answers.answer_text:
## Position

The transport question has a settled answer: QUIC sits directly on UDP and carries its own security and reliability, which is why a middlebox that understands TCP cannot inspect it the same way [1]. HTTP/3 is then the HTTP mapping over that transport [2].

Two details that get lost in summaries:

- *UDP* here does not imply unreliable delivery to the application. QUIC rebuilds ordered, reliable streams on top of datagrams [1].
- Connection migration across a network change is a transport feature, not an HTTP feature [1].

**Deployment caveat.** Some networks rate-limit or block UDP/443 outright, and clients fall back to the older version over TCP in that case [2].

What remains genuinely uncertain is the share of traffic on each version in any specific network, which is an operational measurement rather than a specification fact.

sources.title: RFC 9000: QUIC: A UDP-Based Multiplexed and Secure Transport

initial_answers.answer_text:
**Summary.** HTTP/3 = HTTP semantics over QUIC; QUIC = a UDP-based, encrypted, multiplexed transport [1] [3].

**Detail.** The mapping specification defines the frame layer, the settings exchange and QPACK header compression for the new transport [1]. The prior version defined a comparable frame layer over one TCP connection, and its head-of-line blocking is a property of that single ordered byte stream [2]. Because the transport provides independent stream ordering, that particular stall disappears [3].

**Limits of this answer.** I am describing the specifications as published. Whether a given deployment realises the benefit depends on the loss and reordering profile of the path, which is not something the documents can tell you.

sources.title: RFC 9000: QUIC: A UDP-Based Multiplexed and Secure Transport

final_synthesis.consensus: All four models agree: HTTP/3 carries HTTP semantics over QUIC, and QUIC is a UDP-based transport that supplies its own reliability, congestion control and TLS 1.3 handshake. The change relative to the earlier version is that per-stream ordering removes connection-wide head-of-line blocking, and header compression moved to QPACK. See [RFC 9114](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9114) for the mapping and [RFC 9000](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9000) for the transport.
final_synthesis.disagreement: There is no material disagreement on the substance. The models differ in emphasis: two foreground the framing and header-compression changes, two foreground middlebox and fallback behaviour on networks that restrict UDP.
final_synthesis.source_support: Three specifications carried the load: the HTTP/3 mapping, the QUIC transport document, and [RFC 9113](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9113) for the version being compared against. Every claim above traces to one of them.
final_synthesis.uncertainty: None of the answers can tell you what share of traffic in your own network already uses the newer version, or how much of a published latency win is attributable to the transport as against co-deployed tuning. Deployment behaviour on UDP-restricted paths was flagged by one model only.
final_synthesis.recommendation: Use this as decision support: if you are planning an enablement, measure on your own paths and keep the TCP fallback path exercised rather than assuming the specification result transfers.
